utterances: I also think that the National Black Women's Health Project has been on the case with this for really 20 years, and I want to just celebrate the work that they've been doing.
And then what Marna talked about as well, about finding voice.
One of the women I interviewed said I didn't have any feelings about how I felt when I ate.
I didn't know that there were any feelings there because for years they were stripped away from me.

So for me, a first step in that process is talking with people about how to develop an affectionate understanding for the way that they did cope, not from a place of blame, but from a place of self-love.
Well, Becky, Divya, Marna, thank you so much.
Thank you for having us.
Thank you.
Thank you.
Marna Clowney-Robinson is on the board of directors of the National Association of Anorexia and Associated Disorders.
She joined us from WUOM in Ann Arbor, Michigan.
Divya Kakaiya is a licensed psychologist in San Diego.
And Becky Thompson is a professor of sociology who wrote ""A Hunger So Wide and So Deep.
Some songwriters are able to write songs about those turns in life too painful to talk about.

When we met Merle Haggard aboard his tour bus in the early summer of 2001, he no longer looked much like the hard-scrabble outlaw of his 20s, but a man who'd been tempered by the ups and downs of riches and bankruptcy, love, loss and time.

He'd come a long distance since his boyhood in a converted boxcar on the wrong side of the tracks, growing up just outside of Bakersfield, Calif., and the troubled times when he served more than two years in San Quentin for robbery.

But once Merle Haggard began to sing instead of steal for a living, he became the living link to country music before it went Hollywood, when it was the music of long, hard days on farms and factories and long, lonely nights looking up at an orange moon.
Merle Haggard told us on his tour bus, I don't like music that has no message of any kind.
But I thought his songs didn't send messages so much as they shared what poured from his heart, unfiltered and lyrical.
We spoke to him about his recent album, ""If I Could Only Fly,"" in 2001.
One song, ""I'm Still Your Daddy,"" recollected how a father tells his son that he was once in prison, a conversation Merle Haggard had to have with his children.
Another song, ""Think About A Lullaby,"" was suggested to Merle Haggard by his wife, Theresa Lane, after they lost a child, a little girl, at birth.
But my wife said we should write her a lullaby, not a funeral song, he told us.
It is a song to send love to a child who didn't quite make it into this world but would never leave Merle Haggard's heart and dreams.
I like to think he's singing it to her now.
(SOUNDBITE OF SONG, ""THINK ABOUT A LULLABY"")
(Singing) Think about a lullaby.
Think about a lullaby.
Baby, close your eyes.
Don't cry.
Think about a lullaby.
Let me sing you off to sleep.
Let Daddy pray your soul to keep.
It's safe here with your mom and I.
Think about a lullaby.
From NPR News, this is Day to Day.
A lot of us have the day off tomorrow for July Fourth.
More people than usual, and not necessarily by choice.
The government reports today that June was the sixth straight month of nationwide job losses.
Marketplace's Janet Babin is here now.
And Janet, what reasons are the companies giving for shedding their jobs?

Well, you know, it's just a really weak economy right now, and a lot of companies, Madeleine, are getting hit with all kinds of operating expenses - transportation costs are up, raw materials, in many cases, cost more.
So in this type of economic climate, a lot of companies just aren't hiring and in some cases, they're laying workers off.
Overall, the economy in June lost 62,000 jobs.
And if you look at that number for the year, it's upwards of 400,000 jobs.
Thank goodness for the government, though.
Twenty-nine thousand government jobs created last month.
And there were also modest gains in a couple of sectors - education, health and leisure.

But if you look at, like, construction jobs dropped off, and that's obviously a reflection of the dismal housing market, and manufacturing jobs were down and so did the professional sector lose jobs, and that includes positions like financial services, or real estate, that sort of thing.
What about the service sector?
What about restaurants, hotels, you mentioned leisure, banks.
Are they...
Right.
Yeah.
Leisure is kind of in a grey area.

But if you look at the services sector according to a new report, a separate report out today from the Institute of Supply Management, it calculates that for the services sector overall, there was a drop, an unexpected drop in June.
According to the ISM report, it dropped to 48.2.
And in the numbers that are in this report or the survey, if that number is below 50, which it is, 48.2, then it indicates a contraction.
So there was an unexpected contraction also in services which you wouldn't expect for June.
And what about for people who do have jobs, who are working?
What about their wages?
Are they keeping pace with inflation?
Are they increasing?
Well, overall, the past - yeah, the past 12 months earning have risen about three point four percent.
But inflation's up too, so real wages are falling.
I spoke to Richard DeKaser about this.
He's chief economist at National City Corporation.
And he did find a silver lining in here for me, even though our wages are not going up.
There is a sort of twisted virtue in what's been happening in the labor market.
Labor costs are moderating.
That's of course bad for labor but good for inflation.
That restraint in labor costs will serve to prevent inflationary pressures from getting out of hand.
So inflation's less of a worry because we just aren't going up.
So what about this idea of a recession?
I mean, six straight months of job losses.
Does that mean we're in a recession?
Yeah, it still depends on who you ask, depending on which economist you ask, Madeleine.
OK.
Thank you, Janet.
That's Janet Babin of public radio's daily business show, Marketplace.
So an example would be that one of the cardinal symptoms of post-traumatic stress disorder is hypervigilance - being very jumpy, constantly on guard.
And we found that some of the genes that we know are responsible for vigilance, for jumpiness, are among the genes that have these epigenetic changes associated with the traumatic event experience.
How can you know if these molecular differences between people with and without PTSD cause the disorder, rather than result from the disorder?
It's a challenge to tease apart whether these molecular changes are -cause the disorder or are the result of the disorder.
We have a number of analyses in the paper that show, for example, that the more traumatic events people experience, the more of these molecular changes they have.
That, of course, suggests that the molecular changes are a result of the traumatic events, not the other way around.
Sandro Galea, what do you think all of this research could mean for the way post-traumatic stress disorder might be understood, and also treated in the future?
The closest we are to is to think of these molecular changes as a marker of PTSD.
And it is far from here to treatment.
But at the same time, understanding how it is that PTSD comes about is what ultimately will lead to better therapeutic interventions.
Sandro Galea's study on the biology of PTSD was published online this week in the ""Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences."" He joined us from Columbia University.
Sandro Galea, thank you so much.
Thank you very much for having me.
We continue our series on drought and water today with an unelected official in Las Vegas.
Her name is Patricia Mulroy, and here's what local friends and foes say about her.
Unidentified Man #1: Determined.
Unidentified Man #2: Politically effective.
Unidentified Woman #1: She's tough.
Unidentified Woman #2: Incredibly intimidating.
Unidentified Man #3: She is driven.
And here's how she describes herself.
The worst combination of Irish temper and German stubbornness.
I'm very passionate.
Patricia Mulroy is known as the Water Czar, the Water Empress, even the Water Witch of Las Vegas.
She likes none of those labels, by the way.
Most people call her Pat, and she's the head of the Southern Nevada Water Authority.
Yesterday we heard about ranchers north of the city who say Vegas wants too much water.
Today NPR's Ted Robbins reports how Pat Mulroy has been able to bring lots of water to her city in a desert.
Like anyone who sees a mirage, Patricia Mulroy sees water where there is none.
The difference is, she makes the water appear.
But back in 1989, when she was appointed general manager of the Las Vegas Valley Water District, she saw a city in desert denial.
Well, the valley was predominantly quarter-acre lots or larger, completely surrounded by grass.
And we had consumptions around 340-350 per capita.
That was 350 gallons per person per day - almost twice the water consumption of New York City.
And New York gets 10 times the moisture.
Las Vegas was using water like it was drunk on it.
In 1990, the newly aptly named Mirage Resort on the Vegas Strip spewed water and fire from a volcano.
A new residential development called The Lakes featured homes around three miles of artificial shoreline.
The city was growing fast and Pat Mulroy needed to slow demand.
So she did the unthinkable.
She issued a moratorium on new water hook-ups.
She said this is going to come to a crashing end.
That's Alan Feldman, at the time a vice president with the Mirage.
He says Mulroy told casino owners and developers if things didn't change, the city would run out of water by 2006.
She framed it as a business issue.
This is a resource, this is how much we have, this is its correlation to the economy.
How do we manage it to its best impact?
First, Pat Mulroy brought together local water companies under the umbrella agency she now heads - the Southern Nevada Water Authority.
Next, she started pushing water conservation with promotions like this.
(Soundbite of radio ad)
Unidentified Man #4: This is America's most watered, the radio show that let's you catch the dribs who have been stealing our valley's most precious resource - water.
